title: Second Italo-Ethiopian War

infobox.value: ([Amharic](/wiki/Amharic_language "Amharic language"): ጣልያን ወረራ, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Amharic "Romanization of Amharic"): *Ṭalyan warära*), and in Italy as the **Ethiopian War** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Guerra d'Etiopia*). It is seen as an example of the expansionist policy that characterized the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") and the ineffectiveness of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") before the outbreak of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

The **Second Italo-Ethiopian War**, also referred to as the **Second Italo-Abyssinian War**, was a [war of aggression](/wiki/War_of_aggression "War of aggression") waged by [Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") against [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopian_Empire "Ethiopian Empire"), which lasted from October 1935 to February 1937. In Ethiopia it is often referred to simply as the **Italian Invasion** ([Amharic](/wiki/Amharic_language "Amharic language"): ጣልያን ወረራ, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Amharic "Romanization of Amharic"): *Ṭalyan warära*), and in Italy as the **Ethiopian War** ([Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language"): *Guerra d'Etiopia*). It is seen as an example of the expansionist policy that characterized the [Axis powers](/wiki/Axis_powers "Axis powers") and the ineffectiveness of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") before the outbreak of [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II").

On 3 October 1935, two hundred thousand soldiers of the Italian Army commanded by Marshal [Emilio De Bono](/wiki/Emilio_De_Bono "Emilio De Bono") attacked from [Eritrea](/wiki/Italian_Eritrea "Italian Eritrea") (then an Italian colonial possession) without prior declaration of war.[[13]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeckie1987645-14) At the same time a smaller force under General [Rodolfo Graziani](/wiki/Rodolfo_Graziani "Rodolfo Graziani") attacked from [Italian Somalia](/wiki/Italian_Somalia "Italian Somalia"). On 6 October, [Adwa](/wiki/Adwa "Adwa") was conquered, a symbolic place for the Italian army because of the defeat at the [Battle of Adwa](/wiki/Battle_of_Adwa "Battle of Adwa") by the Ethiopian army during the [First Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/First_Italo-Ethiopian_War "First Italo-Ethiopian War"). On 15 October, Italian troops seized [Aksum](/wiki/Aksum "Aksum"), and an [obelisk](/wiki/Obelisk_of_Axum "Obelisk of Axum") adorning the city was torn from its site and sent to Rome to be placed symbolically in front of the building of the [Ministry of Colonies](/wiki/Ministry_of_the_Colonies_(Italy) "Ministry of the Colonies (Italy)").

Exasperated by De Bono's slow and cautious progress, Italian prime minister [Benito Mussolini](/wiki/Benito_Mussolini "Benito Mussolini") replaced him with General [Pietro Badoglio](/wiki/Pietro_Badoglio "Pietro Badoglio"). Ethiopian forces attacked the newly arrived invading army and launched a [counterattack](/wiki/Christmas_Offensive "Christmas Offensive") in December 1935, but their poorly armed forces could not resist for long against the modern weapons of the Italians. Frustrated, Mussolini authorized the use of mustard gas, with the *[Regia Aeronautica](/wiki/Regia_Aeronautica "Regia Aeronautica")* (Royal Air Force) dropping 330 tons over the next four months, violating the 1925 [Geneva Protocol](/wiki/Geneva_Protocol "Geneva Protocol"). [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") sent arms and munitions to Ethiopia because it was frustrated over Italian objections to its attempts to integrate Austria.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeckie198764-15) This prolonged the war and sapped Italian resources. It would soon lead to Italy's greater economic dependence on Germany and less interventionist policy on Austria, clearing the path for [Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler")'s *[Anschluss](/wiki/Anschluss "Anschluss")*.[[15]](#cite_note-16)

The Ethiopian counteroffensive managed to stop the Italian advance for a few weeks, but the superiority of the Italians' weapons (particularly heavy [artillery](/wiki/Artillery "Artillery") and airstrikes with bombs and [chemical weapons](/wiki/Chemical_weapon "Chemical weapon")) prevented the Ethiopians from taking advantage of their initial successes. The Italians resumed the offensive in early February, decisively defeating the Ethiopian armies at [Amba Aradam](/wiki/Battle_of_Amba_Aradam "Battle of Amba Aradam"), [Tembien](/wiki/Second_battle_of_Tembien "Second battle of Tembien"), and [Shire](/wiki/Battle_of_Shire_(1936) "Battle of Shire (1936)"). On 31 March 1936, the Italians won a decisive victory at the [Battle of Maychew](/wiki/Battle_of_Maychew "Battle of Maychew"), which nullified any possible organized Ethiopian resistance. Emperor [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") was forced to escape into exile on 2 May, and Badoglio's forces arrived in the capital [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa") on 5 May. Mussolini then announced the annexation of Ethiopia on 9 May and the Italian King [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel III of Italy") was proclaimed emperor. The provinces of Eritrea, Italian Somaliland and Abyssinia (Ethiopia) were united to form [Italian East Africa](/wiki/Italian_East_Africa "Italian East Africa") on 1 June. Fighting between Italian and Ethiopian troops persisted until 19 February 1937.[[16]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMockler2003172–73-17) On the same day, an attempted assassination of Graziani led to the reprisal [Yekatit 12](/wiki/Yekatit_12 "Yekatit 12") massacre in Addis Ababa, in which between 1,400 and 30,000 civilians were killed.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-18)[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968292–293-19)[[19]](#cite_note-20) Italian forces continued to suppress rebel activity by the *[Arbegnoch](/wiki/Arbegnoch "Arbegnoch")* for many years after.[[20]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968281,_300-21)

Italian troops used [mustard gas](/wiki/Mustard_gas "Mustard gas") in aerial bombardments (in violation of the [Geneva Protocol](/wiki/Geneva_Protocol "Geneva Protocol") and [Geneva Conventions](/wiki/Geneva_Conventions "Geneva Conventions")) against combatants and civilians in an attempt to suppress Ethiopian resistance.[[21]](#cite_note-22)[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1983231,_417-23) Deliberate Italian attacks against ambulances and hospitals of the [Red Cross](/wiki/International_Red_Cross_and_Red_Crescent_Movement "International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement") were reported.[[23]](#cite_note-rain-24) By all estimates, hundreds of thousands of Ethiopian civilians died as a result of the Italian invasion, which have been described by some historians as constituting [genocide](/wiki/Genocide "Genocide").[[24]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELabanca2004300–313-25) Crimes by Ethiopian troops included the use of [dumdum bullets](/wiki/Expanding_bullet "Expanding bullet") (in violation of the [Hague Conventions](/wiki/Hague_Conventions_of_1899_and_1907 "Hague Conventions of 1899 and 1907")), the killing of civilian workmen (including during the [Gondrand massacre](/wiki/Gondrand_massacre "Gondrand massacre")) and the mutilation of captured [Eritrean Ascari](/wiki/Eritrean_Ascari "Eritrean Ascari") and Italians (often with castration), beginning in the first weeks of war.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESbacchi197843-26)[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAntonicelli197579-27)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

### State of East Africa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: State of East Africa")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5f/Italian_empire_1914.png/250px-Italian_empire_1914.png)](/wiki/File:Italian_empire_1914.png)

Italy and its [colonial possessions](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire") at the time of the outbreak of [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I")

The [Kingdom of Italy](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Italy "Kingdom of Italy") began its attempts to establish colonies in the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa") in the 1880s. The first phase of the colonial expansion concluded with the disastrous [First Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/First_Italo-Ethiopian_War "First Italo-Ethiopian War") and the defeat of the Italian forces in the [Battle of Adwa](/wiki/Battle_of_Adwa "Battle of Adwa"), on 1 March 1896, inflicted by the Ethiopian Army of [Negus](/wiki/Negus "Negus") [Menelik II](/wiki/Menelik_II "Menelik II").[[27]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELabanca200428–66-28) In the following years, Italy abandoned its expansionist plans in the area and limited itself to administering the small possessions that it retained in the area: the colony of [Italian Eritrea](/wiki/Italian_Eritrea "Italian Eritrea") and the protectorate (later colony) of [Italian Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_Somaliland "Italian Somaliland"). For the next few decades, Italian-Ethiopian economic and diplomatic relations remained relatively stable.[[28]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDominioni20087-29)

On 14 December 1925, Italy's fascist government signed a secret pact with Britain aimed at reinforcing Italian dominance in the region. London recognised that the area was of Italian interest and agreed to the Italian request to build a railway connecting Somalia and Eritrea. Although the signatories had wished to maintain the secrecy of the agreement, the plan soon leaked and caused indignation by the French and Ethiopian governments. The latter denounced it as a betrayal of a country that had been for all intents and purposes a member of the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations").[[29]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELabanca2004146–148-30)

As fascist rule in Italy continued to radicalise, its colonial governors in the Horn of Africa began pushing outward the margins of their imperial foothold. The governor of Italian Eritrea, [Jacopo Gasparini](/wiki/Jacopo_Gasparini "Jacopo Gasparini"), focused on the exploitation of [Teseney](/wiki/Teseney "Teseney") in an attempt to win over the leaders of the [Tigre people](/wiki/Tigre_people "Tigre people") against Ethiopia. The governor of Italian Somaliland, [Cesare Maria de Vecchi](/wiki/Cesare_Maria_De_Vecchi "Cesare Maria De Vecchi"), began a policy of repression that led to the occupation of the fertile [Jubaland](/wiki/Jubaland "Jubaland"), and the cessation in 1928 of collaboration between the settlers and the traditional Somali chiefs.

### Walwal Incident

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Walwal Incident")]

Main article: [Abyssinia Crisis](/wiki/Abyssinia_Crisis "Abyssinia Crisis")

The [Italo-Ethiopian Treaty of 1928](/wiki/Italo-Ethiopian_Treaty_of_1928 "Italo-Ethiopian Treaty of 1928") stated that the border between Italian Somaliland and Ethiopia was 21 [leagues](/wiki/League_(unit) "League (unit)") parallel to the [Benadir](/wiki/Benadir "Benadir") coast (approximately 118.3 kilometres [73.5 miles]). In 1930, Italy built a fort at the [Welwel](/wiki/Welwel,_Ethiopia "Welwel, Ethiopia") oasis (also *Walwal*, Italian: *Ual-Ual*) in the [Ogaden](/wiki/Ogaden "Ogaden") and garrisoned it with Somali [dubats](/wiki/Dubats "Dubats") (irregular frontier troops commanded by Italian officers). The fort at Welwel was well beyond the 21-league limit and inside Ethiopian territory. On 23 November 1934, an Anglo–Ethiopian boundary commission studying grazing grounds to find a definitive border between British Somaliland and Ethiopia arrived at Welwel.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker19681–6-31)

The party contained Ethiopian and British technicians and an escort of around 600 Ethiopian soldiers. Both sides knew that the Italians had installed a military post at Welwel and were not surprised to see an [Italian flag](/wiki/Italian_flag "Italian flag") at the wells. The Ethiopian government had notified the Italian authorities in Italian Somaliland that the commission was active in the Ogaden and requested the Italians to co-operate. When the British commissioner Lieutenant-Colonel [Esmond Clifford](/wiki/Esmond_Clifford "Esmond Clifford"), asked the Italians for permission to camp nearby, the Italian commander, Captain Roberto Cimmaruta, rebuffed the request.[[30]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker19681–6-31)

Fitorari Shiferra, the commander of the Ethiopian escort, took no notice of the 150 Italian and Somali troops and made camp. To avoid being caught in an Italian–Ethiopian incident, Clifford withdrew the British contingent to Ado, about 20 mi (32 km) to the north-east, and Italian aircraft began to fly over Welwel. The Ethiopian commissioners retired with the British, but the escort remained. For ten days both sides exchanged menaces, sometimes no more than 2 m apart. Reinforcements increased the Ethiopian contingent to about 1,500 men and the Italians to about 500, and on 5 December 1934, shots were fired. The Italians were supported by an armoured car and bomber aircraft. The bombs missed, but machine gunfire from the car caused about 110 Ethiopian casualties.[[31]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker19686–7-32) 30 to 50 Italians and Somalis were killed. The incident led to the [Abyssinia Crisis](/wiki/Abyssinia_Crisis "Abyssinia Crisis") at the League of Nations.[[32]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMockler200346-33) On 4 September 1935, the League of Nations exonerated both parties for the incident.[[33]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShinnOfcansky2013392-34)

### Ethiopian isolation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ethiopian isolation")]

Britain and France, preferring Italy as an ally against Germany, did not take strong steps to discourage an Italian military buildup on the borders of [Italian Eritrea](/wiki/Italian_Eritrea "Italian Eritrea") and [Italian Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_Somaliland "Italian Somaliland"). Because of the [German Question](/wiki/German_question "German question"), Mussolini needed to deter Hitler from annexing [Austria](/wiki/Austria "Austria") while much of the Italian Army was being deployed to the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa"), which led him to draw closer to France to provide the necessary deterrent.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000126-35) King [Victor Emmanuel III](/wiki/Victor_Emmanuel_III_of_Italy "Victor Emmanuel III of Italy") shared the traditional Italian respect for British sea power and insisted to Mussolini that Italy must not antagonise Britain before he [assented](/wiki/Royal_assent "Royal assent") to the war.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000126-35) In that regard, British diplomacy in the first half of 1935 greatly assisted Mussolini's efforts to win Victor Emmanuel's support for the invasion.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000126-35)

On 7 January 1935, a [Franco-Italian Agreement](/wiki/Franco-Italian_Agreement "Franco-Italian Agreement") was made that gave Italy essentially a free hand in [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") in return for Italian co-operation in [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe").[[35]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStearnsLanger2002677-36) [Pierre Laval](/wiki/Pierre_Laval "Pierre Laval") told Mussolini that he wanted a Franco-Italian alliance against [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") and that Italy had a "free hand" in Ethiopia.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000126-35) In April, Italy was further emboldened by participation in the [Stresa Front](/wiki/Stresa_Front "Stresa Front"), an agreement to curb further German violations of the [Treaty of Versailles](/wiki/Treaty_of_Versailles "Treaty of Versailles").[[36]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECrozier2004108-37) The first draft of the communique at Stresa Summit spoke of upholding stability all over the world, but British Foreign Secretary, [Sir John Simon](/wiki/Sir_John_Simon "Sir John Simon"), insisted for the final draft to declare that Britain, France and Italy were committed to upholding stability "in Europe", which Mussolini took for British acceptance of an invasion of Ethiopia.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000126-35)

In June, non-interference was further assured by a political rift, which had developed between the United Kingdom and France, because of the [Anglo-German Naval Agreement](/wiki/Anglo-German_Naval_Agreement "Anglo-German Naval Agreement").[[37]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStackelberg2009164-38) As 300,000 Italian soldiers were transferred to Eritrea and Italian Somaliland over the spring and the summer of 1935, the world's media was abuzz with speculation that Italy would soon be invading Ethiopia.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000126-35) In June 1935, [Anthony Eden](/wiki/Anthony_Eden "Anthony Eden") arrived in Rome with the message that Britain opposed an invasion and had a compromise plan for Italy to be given a corridor in Ethiopia to link the two Italian colonies in the [Horn of Africa](/wiki/Horn_of_Africa "Horn of Africa"), which Mussolini rejected outright.[[34]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000126-35) As the Italians had broken the British naval codes, Mussolini knew of the problems in the British Mediterranean Fleet, which led him to believe that the British opposition to the invasion, which had come as an unwelcome surprise to him, was not serious and that Britain would never go to war over Ethiopia.[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000127-39)

The prospect that an Italian invasion of Ethiopia would cause a crisis in Anglo-Italian relations was seen as an opportunity in [Berlin](/wiki/Berlin "Berlin"). Although Hitler did not want to see [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") win, Germany provided some weapons to Ethiopia out of fear of quick victory for Italy.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000145-40) The German perspective was that if Italy was bogged down in a long war in Ethiopia, that would probably lead to Britain pushing the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") to impose sanctions on Italy, which the French would almost certainly not veto out of fear of destroying relations with Britain; that would cause a crisis in Anglo-Italian relations and allow Germany to offer its "good services" to Italy.[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000145-40) In that way, Hitler hoped to win Mussolini as an ally and to destroy the [Stresa Front](/wiki/Stresa_Front "Stresa Front").[[39]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000145-40)

A final possible foreign ally of Ethiopia [was Japan](/wiki/Ethiopia%E2%80%93Japan_relations "Ethiopia–Japan relations"), which had served as a model to some Ethiopian intellectuals. After the Welwel incident, several right-wing Japanese groups, including the Great Asianism Association and the [Black Dragon Society](/wiki/Black_Dragon_Society "Black Dragon Society"), attempted to raise money for the Ethiopian cause. The Japanese ambassador to Italy, Dr. Sugimura Yotaro, on 16 July assured Mussolini that Japan held no political interests in Ethiopia and would stay neutral in the coming war. His comments stirred up a furor inside [Japan](/wiki/Japan "Japan"), where there had been popular affinity for the fellow nonwhite empire in Africa,[[40]](#cite_note-:1-41) which was reciprocated with similar anger in Italy towards Japan combined with praise for Mussolini and his firm stance against the "gialli di Tokyo" ("Tokyo Yellows").[[40]](#cite_note-:1-41) Despite popular opinion, when the Ethiopians approached Japan for help on 2 August, they were refused, and even a modest request for the Japanese government for an official statement of its support for Ethiopia during the coming conflict was denied.[[41]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECalvitt_Clarke19999–20-42)

## Armies

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Armies")]

### Ethiopian forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Ethiopian forces")]

Main articles: [Ethiopian order of battle in the Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Ethiopian_order_of_battle_in_the_Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Ethiopian order of battle in the Second Italo-Ethiopian War") and [List of Second Italo-Ethiopian War weapons of Ethiopia](/wiki/List_of_Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War_weapons_of_Ethiopia "List of Second Italo-Ethiopian War weapons of Ethiopia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Addis_Ababa%2C_Ethiopia._Medical_supplies_for_the_front%2C_including_bales_of_cotton.jpg/250px-Addis_Ababa%2C_Ethiopia._Medical_supplies_for_the_front%2C_including_bales_of_cotton.jpg)](/wiki/File:Addis_Ababa,_Ethiopia._Medical_supplies_for_the_front,_including_bales_of_cotton.jpg)

Medical supplies for the front in Addis Ababa

On the eve of hostilities, the Ethiopians had mobilized an army of 350,000–760,000 men equipped with repeating rifles of various models and calibers, though mostly modern, with approximately 150 rounds of ammunition per soldier. As war appeared inevitable, [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") began to accelerate the rearmament and "spend every last cent to defend the integrity of Ethiopia", making available his entire personal fortune. From January to July 1935, Ethiopia was able to import large numbers of weapons from Europe, before the embargo decreed by the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") came into force, approximately 16,000 rifles, 600 light machine guns and half a million bullets.[[42]](#cite_note-43) The arms embargo imposed on the belligerents by France and Britain disproportionately affected Ethiopia, which lacked the manufacturing industry to produce its own weapons. Only Germany, which hoped to keep Italy busy in Africa for as long as possible in order to ease its pressure on [Austria](/wiki/First_Austrian_Republic "First Austrian Republic"), continued to secretly supply Ethiopia with arms and ammunition.[[43]](#cite_note-44) [Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") sent three aeroplanes, 10,000 [Mauser rifles](/wiki/Mauser_rifles "Mauser rifles") and 10 million rounds of ammunition to the Ethiopians.[[44]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEStapleton2013203-45)

The army had about 800 light [Colt](/wiki/M1895_Colt%E2%80%93Browning_machine_gun "M1895 Colt–Browning machine gun") and [Hotchkiss](/wiki/Hotchkiss_M1922_machine_gun "Hotchkiss M1922 machine gun") machine-guns and 250 heavy [Vickers](/wiki/Vickers_machine_gun "Vickers machine gun") and [Hotchkiss](/wiki/Hotchkiss_M1914_machine_gun "Hotchkiss M1914 machine gun") machine guns, about 100 .303-inch Vickers guns on AA mounts, 48 [20 mm Oerlikon S](/wiki/Oerlikon_20_mm_cannon "Oerlikon 20 mm cannon") [anti-aircraft guns](/wiki/Anti-aircraft_gun "Anti-aircraft gun") and some recently purchased [Canon de 75 CA modèle 1917 Schneider](/wiki/Canon_de_75_antia%C3%A9rien_mle_1913-1917 "Canon de 75 antiaérien mle 1913-1917") 75 mm field guns. The Ethiopian army had some 300 [trucks](/wiki/Truck "Truck"), seven [Ford A](/wiki/Ford_Model_A_(1927%E2%80%9331) "Ford Model A (1927–31)")-based [armoured cars](/wiki/Armored_car_(military) "Armored car (military)") and four World War I–era [Fiat 3000](/wiki/Fiat_3000 "Fiat 3000") tanks.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197129-6)

The best Ethiopian units were the emperor's "[Kebur Zabagna](/wiki/Kebur_Zabagna "Kebur Zabagna")" (Imperial Guard), which were well-trained and better equipped than the other Ethiopian troops. The Imperial Guard wore a distinctive greenish-khaki uniform of the [Belgian Army](/wiki/Belgian_Land_Component "Belgian Land Component"), which stood out from the white cotton cloak (*shamma*), which was worn by most Ethiopian fighters and proved to be an excellent target.[[5]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197129-6) The skills of the *[Rases](/wiki/Ras_(title) "Ras (title)")*, the Ethiopian generals, were reported to rate from relatively good to incompetent. Haile Selassie ordered the use of guerrilla tactics, but for cultural reasons this would be impracticable. His commanders considered guerrilla warfare to be dishonorable, so they instead preferred to engage the Italians in [frontal battles](/wiki/Frontal_assault "Frontal assault").[[45]](#cite_note-46)

The serviceable portion of the [Ethiopian Air Force](/wiki/Ethiopian_Air_Force "Ethiopian Air Force") was commanded by a Frenchman, André Maillet, and included three obsolete [Potez 25](/wiki/Potez_25 "Potez 25") biplanes.[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197157-47) A few transport aircraft had been acquired between 1934 and 1935 for ambulance work, but the Air Force had 13 aircraft and four pilots at the outbreak of the war.[[7]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEShinnOfcansky201319-8) [Airspeed](/wiki/Airspeed_Ltd "Airspeed Ltd") in England had a surplus [Viceroy](/wiki/Airspeed_Viceroy "Airspeed Viceroy") racing plane, and its director, [Neville Shute](/wiki/Neville_Shute "Neville Shute"), was delighted with a good offer for the "[white elephant](/wiki/White_elephant "White elephant")" in August 1935. The agent said that it was to fly cinema films around Europe. When the client wanted bomb racks to carry the (flammable) films, Shute agreed to fit lugs under the wings to which they could attach "anything they liked". He was told that the plane was to be used to bomb the Italian oil storage tanks at Massawa, and when the CID enquired about the alien (ex-German) pilot practices in it Shute got the impression that the Foreign Office did not object. However, fuel, bombs and bomb racks from Finland could not reach Ethiopia in time, and the paid-for Viceroy stayed at its works. The emperor of Ethiopia had £16,000 to spend on modern aircraft to resist the Italians and planned to spend £5000 on the Viceroy and the rest on three [Gloster Gladiator](/wiki/Gloster_Gladiator "Gloster Gladiator") fighters.[[47]](#cite_note-48)

There were 50 foreign mercenaries who joined the Ethiopian forces, including French pilots like Pierre Corriger, American pilot [John Robinson](/wiki/John_Robinson_(aviator) "John Robinson (aviator)"), the Trinidadian pilot [Hubert Julian](/wiki/Hubert_Julian "Hubert Julian"), an official Swedish military mission under Captain [Viking Tamm](/wiki/Viking_Tamm "Viking Tamm"), the [White Russian](/wiki/White_movement "White movement") Feodor Konovalov and the Czechoslovak writer Adolf Parlesak. Several Austrian Nazis, a team of Belgian fascists, and the Cuban mercenary Alejandro del Valle also fought for Haile Selassie.[[48]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOthen2017238–239-49) Many of the individuals were military advisers, pilots, doctors or supporters of the Ethiopian cause; 50 mercenaries fought in the Ethiopian army and another 50 people were active in the Ethiopian [Red Cross](/wiki/Red_Cross "Red Cross") or nonmilitary activities.[[49]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEOthen2017238-50) The Italians later attributed most of the relative success achieved by the Ethiopians to foreigners, or *ferenghi*.[[50]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicolle199718-51) (The Italian propaganda machine magnified the number to thousands to explain away the [Ethiopian Christmas Offensive](/wiki/Ethiopian_Christmas_Offensive "Ethiopian Christmas Offensive") in late 1935.)[[51]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197147-52)

A former [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") general named [Wehib Pasha](/wiki/Wehib_Pasha "Wehib Pasha") also served as a military advisor with the Ethiopian army during the war, notably designing a defensive line for Ethiopian troops known as the "Hindenburg Wall", which was broken through by Italian troops during the [Battle of the Ogaden](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Ogaden "Battle of the Ogaden") in 1936.

### Italian forces

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Italian forces")]

Main articles: [Italian order of battle for the Second Italo-Ethiopian War](/wiki/Italian_order_of_battle_for_the_Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Italian order of battle for the Second Italo-Ethiopian War") and [List of Second Italo-Ethiopian War weapons of Italy](/wiki/List_of_Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War_weapons_of_Italy "List of Second Italo-Ethiopian War weapons of Italy")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8c/Soldatietiopia.jpg/250px-Soldatietiopia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Soldatietiopia.jpg)

Italian soldiers recruited in 1935 in [Montevarchi](/wiki/Montevarchi "Montevarchi") to fight the Second Italo-Abyssinian War

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/71/Modern%2C_Military_medal_%28FindID_383905%29.jpg/250px-Modern%2C_Military_medal_%28FindID_383905%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Modern,_Military_medal_(FindID_383905).jpg)

Medal commemorating the role of the Italian Eritrean colonial troops in the war

In April 1935, the mobilization of the [Royal Italian Army](/wiki/Royal_Italian_Army "Royal Italian Army") (*Regio Esercito*) and the *[Regia Aeronautica](/wiki/Regia_Aeronautica "Regia Aeronautica")* (Royal Air Force) in East Africa (*Africa Orientale*) accelerated. The mobilization of men and resources was a significant achievement for Italy and, despite the limited time available, was carried out without major difficulties. It reached extraordinary proportions, being regarded as the largest colonial war ever in terms of manpower, the scale and modernity of its resources, and the speed of its preparation. The classes from 1911 to 1915 were called to arms, which allowed the army to have an enormous availability of men without weakening the army at home. Between February 1935 and January 1936, six army divisions were sent to Eritrea and one to Somalia.[[52]](#cite_note-53)

At Mussolini's request, the [Voluntary Militia for National Security](/wiki/Voluntary_Militia_for_National_Security "Voluntary Militia for National Security") (Blackshirts) became an important component of the expeditionary force, intended to represent the fascist character of the campaign; thanks to the approximately 80,000 volunteers who showed up (including those not used by the army) six Blackshirt divisions were sent between August and November 1935 to Eritrea and one to Somalia.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Overall, about 685,000 soldiers and a great number of logistical and support units was sent to East Africa; which included 200 journalists.[[54]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaer197613-55) The Italians had 6,000 machine guns, 2,000 pieces of artillery, 599 tanks and 390 aircraft. The *[Regia Marina](/wiki/Regia_Marina "Regia Marina")* (Royal Navy) carried tons of ammunition, food and other supplies, with the motor vehicles to move them, but the Ethiopians had only horse-drawn carts.[[8]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197120-9)

The Italians placed considerable reliance on their Corps of Colonial Troops (*Regio Corpo Truppe Coloniali*, RCTC) of indigenous regiments recruited from the Italian colonies of Eritrea, Somalia and [Libya](/wiki/Libya "Libya"). The most effective of the Italian commanded units were the Eritrean native infantry (*[Ascari](/wiki/Eritrean_Ascari "Eritrean Ascari")*), which was often used as advanced troops. The Eritreans also provided cavalry and artillery units; the "Falcon Feathers" (*Penne di Falco*) was one prestigious and colourful Eritrean cavalry unit. Other RCTC units during the invasion of Ethiopia were irregular Somali frontier troops (*dubats*), regular Arab-Somali infantry and artillery and infantry from Libya.[[55]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968318-56) The Italians had a variety of local semi-independent "allies" in the north, and the [Azebo Galla](/wiki/Azebo_Galla "Azebo Galla") were among several groups induced to fight for the Italians. In the south, the Somali sultan [Olol Dinle](/wiki/Olol_Dinle "Olol Dinle") commanded a personal army, which advanced into the northern Ogaden with the forces of Colonel [Luigi Frusci](/wiki/Luigi_Frusci "Luigi Frusci"). The Italian colonial forces even included men from [Yemen](/wiki/Yemen "Yemen"), across the [Gulf of Aden](/wiki/Gulf_of_Aden "Gulf of Aden").[[56]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicolle199741-57)

The Italians were reinforced by volunteers from the so-called *Italiani all'estero*, members of the [Italian diaspora](/wiki/Italian_diaspora "Italian diaspora") from [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Uruguay](/wiki/Uruguay "Uruguay") and [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil "Brazil"); they formed the 221st Legion in the *Divisione Tevere*, which a special *Legione Parini* fought under [Luigi Frusci](/wiki/Luigi_Frusci "Luigi Frusci") on the southern front.[[57]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968237,_267-58) The most notable foreign volunteer on the Italian side was the Peruvian [Antonio Cicirello](/w/index.php?title=Antonio_Cicirello&action=edit&redlink=1 "Antonio Cicirello (page does not exist)"), who was posthumously decorated with the [Gold Medal of Military Valour](/wiki/Gold_Medal_of_Military_Valour "Gold Medal of Military Valour"). Most foreigners accompanied the Ethiopians, but [Herbert Matthews](/wiki/Herbert_Matthews "Herbert Matthews"), a reporter and historian who wrote *Eyewitness in Abyssinia: With Marshal Bodoglio's forces to Addis Ababa* (1937), and [Pedro del Valle](/wiki/Pedro_del_Valle "Pedro del Valle"), an observer for [US Marine Corps](/wiki/US_Marine_Corps "US Marine Corps"), accompanied the Italian forces.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968222-59)

On 28 March 1935, General [Emilio De Bono](/wiki/Emilio_De_Bono "Emilio De Bono") was named the commander-in-chief of all Italian armed forces in East Africa.[[59]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGooch2007301-60) De Bono was also the commander-in-chief of the forces invading from Eritrea on the northern front. De Bono commanded nine divisions in the Italian I Corps, the Italian II Corps and the Eritrean Corps. General [Rodolfo Graziani](/wiki/Rodolfo_Graziani "Rodolfo Graziani") was commander-in-chief of forces invading from Italian Somaliland on the southern front.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGooch2007299-61) Initially, he had two divisions and a variety of smaller units under his command: a mixture of Italians, Somalis, Eritreans, Libyans and others. De Bono regarded Italian Somaliland as a secondary theatre, whose primary need was to defend itself, but it could aid the main front with offensive thrusts if the enemy forces were not too large there.[[60]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGooch2007299-61)
