## Hostilities

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Hostilities")]

### Italian invasion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Italian invasion")]

Main articles: [De Bono's invasion of Ethiopia](/wiki/De_Bono%27s_invasion_of_Ethiopia "De Bono's invasion of Ethiopia") and [Christmas Offensive](/wiki/Christmas_Offensive "Christmas Offensive")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/95/Ethiopia_War_Map_%281935-feb_1936%29_it.svg/250px-Ethiopia_War_Map_%281935-feb_1936%29_it.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ethiopia_War_Map_(1935-feb_1936)_it.svg)

A map showing the military actions from 1935 to February 1936

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Bando_contro_lo_schiavismo_in_Tigr%C3%A8.jpg/250px-Bando_contro_lo_schiavismo_in_Tigr%C3%A8.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bando_contro_lo_schiavismo_in_Tigr%C3%A8.jpg)

Italian notice, signed by general [Emilio De Bono](/wiki/Emilio_De_Bono "Emilio De Bono"), proclaiming the abolishment of slavery in [Tigray](/wiki/Tigray_Region "Tigray Region") in [Italian](/wiki/Italian_language "Italian language") and [Amharic](/wiki/Amharic_language "Amharic language"). The abolition of slavery was one of the first measures taken by the Italian occupation government in Ethiopia.

At 5:00 am on 3 October 1935, De Bono crossed the [Mareb River](/wiki/Mareb_River "Mareb River") and advanced into Ethiopia from Eritrea without a [declaration of war](/wiki/Declaration_of_war "Declaration of war").[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197133-62) General De Bono's attack force included I East African Army Corps, under General [Ruggero Santini](/wiki/Ruggero_Santini "Ruggero Santini"), on the left; II East African Army Corps, under Lieutenant General Pietro Maravigna, on the right, and the Eritrean Army Corps (Corpo d'Armata Indigeni), under General [Alessandro Pirzio Biroli](/wiki/Alessandro_Pirzio_Biroli "Alessandro Pirzio Biroli"), in the center. On De Bono's flanks, the eastern lowlands included the 13th and 26th Colonial battalions, a Libyan battalion, and three irregular bands in the regions of Massawa, northern Danakil, and southern Danakil. The western lowlands included the 27th and 28th Colonial battalions, a unit of irregulars and Libyan volunteers supported by the 17th Colonial Battalion.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

At this point in the campaign, the lack of roads represented a serious hindrance for the Italians as they crossed into Ethiopia. On the Eritrean side, roads had been constructed right up to the border. On the Ethiopian side, these roads often transitioned into vaguely defined paths,[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197133-62) and the Italian army used [aerial photography](/wiki/Aerial_reconnaissance_in_World_War_II#Italian_reconnaissance_over_Ethiopia "Aerial reconnaissance in World War II")[[63]](#cite_note-64) to plan its advance. De Bono realized that the Italian lines were over-extended and his forces were incapable of holding a 300-mile long front in a region filled with steep mountains and deep gorges. The early rainy season also had washed out several recently built mountain roads, thus impeding their ability to re-supply troops.

On 5 October the Italian I Corps took [Adigrat](/wiki/Adigrat "Adigrat"), and by 6 October, [Adwa](/wiki/Adwa "Adwa") (Adowa) was captured by the Italian II Corps. Haile Selassie had ordered *Ras* [Seyoum Mangasha](/wiki/Seyum_Mangasha "Seyum Mangasha"), the Commander of the Ethiopian [Army of Tigre](/wiki/Tigray_Province "Tigray Province"), to withdraw a day's march away from the Mareb River. Later, the Emperor ordered his son-in-law and *[Dejazmach](/wiki/Dejazmach "Dejazmach")* [Haile Selassie Gugsa](/wiki/Haile_Selassie_Gugsa "Haile Selassie Gugsa"), also in the area, to move back 89 and 56 km (55 and 35 mi) from the border.[[61]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197133-62)

On 11 October, Gugsa surrendered with 1,200 followers at the Italian outpost at [Idaga Hamus](/wiki/Idaga_Hamus_(Saesi_Tsaedaemba) "Idaga Hamus (Saesi Tsaedaemba)"). Italian propagandists lavishly publicised the surrender but fewer than a tenth of Gugsa's men defected with him.[[64]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197135-65) On 14 October, De Bono proclaimed the end of [slavery in Ethiopia](/wiki/Slavery_in_Ethiopia "Slavery in Ethiopia") and the military reported the emancipation of 16,000 slaves over the next two weeks, which greatly pleased international opinion.[[65]](#cite_note-66) By 15 October, De Bono's forces had advanced from Adwa and occupied the holy capital of [Axum](/wiki/Axum "Axum"). De Bono entered the city riding on a white horse and then looted the [Obelisk of Axum](/wiki/Obelisk_of_Axum "Obelisk of Axum").[[66]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968157-67) To Mussolini's dismay, the advance was methodical and on 8 November, the I Corps and the Eritrean Corps captured [Mekelle](/wiki/Mekelle "Mekelle"). The Italian advance had added 56 mi (90 km) to the line of supply and De Bono wanted to build a road from Adigrat before continuing.[[67]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968170-68)[[68]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197136-69)

In early October 1935, [Rodolfo Graziani](/wiki/Rodolfo_Graziani "Rodolfo Graziani") began the southern campaign by deploying mobile columns of motorized troops supported by tanks and the *Regia Aeronautica*. The Italians faced steady Ethiopian resistance by the troops of [Afawarq Walda Samayat](/wiki/Afawarq_Walda_Samayat "Afawarq Walda Samayat") in the valley of River Fafan, at Gorahai. Only after Afawarq was mortally wounded on 7 November, the Italians captured Gorahai. From Gorahai, the Italians deployed 120 miles up the Fafan valley to [Degehabur](/wiki/Degehabur "Degehabur"). Attacks by the troops of [Nasibu Zeamanuel](/wiki/Nasibu_Zeamanuel "Nasibu Zeamanuel") against Graziani’s rear units forced the column to return to Gorahai. Meanwhile, another Italian column under [Pietro Maletti](/wiki/Pietro_Maletti "Pietro Maletti") advanced to [Degehabur](/wiki/Degehabur "Degehabur") but had to withdraw just north of Gorahai after a deadly ambush. By late 1935, Graziani, disposing over a small number of troops, remained largely on the defensive, also being effectively blocked by the army of Nasibu in [Hararghe](/wiki/Hararghe "Hararghe") and the army of [Desta Damtew](/wiki/Desta_Damtew "Desta Damtew") in [Sidamo](/wiki/Sidamo_Province "Sidamo Province"). Heavy rains also contributed to Graziani’s lack of progress.[[69]](#cite_note-70)

In November 1935, Brigadier General Oreste Mariotti penetrated the [Danakil Desert](/wiki/Danakil_Desert "Danakil Desert") from Kulul after being assembled at the Eritrean port of Fatima Eri. Securing the left flank of the Italian army, Mariotti and his troops marched across the desert, ascended up the Tigrean plateau, where they repelled an attack of *Dejazmach* Kasa Sebhat’s troops on 12–13 November at [Atsbi](/wiki/Atsbi "Atsbi"). The force then continued to [Mekelle](/wiki/Mekelle "Mekelle"), where it joined the troops of I Corps.[[70]](#cite_note-71)

In early December 1935, the [Hoare–Laval Pact](/wiki/Hoare%E2%80%93Laval_Pact "Hoare–Laval Pact") was proposed by Britain and France. Italy would gain the best parts of Ogaden and Tigray and economic influence over all the south. Abyssinia would have a guaranteed corridor to the sea at the port of [Assab](/wiki/Assab "Assab"); the corridor was a poor one and known as a "corridor for camels".[[71]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMockler200375-72) Mussolini was ready to play along with considering the Hoare-Laval Pact, rather than rejecting it outright, to avoid a complete break with Britain and France, but he kept demanding changes to the plan before he would accept it as a way to stall for more time to allow his army to conquer Ethiopia.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000128-73) Mussolini was not prepared to abandon the goal of conquering Ethiopia, but the imposition of League of Nations sanctions on Italy caused much alarm in Rome.[[73]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000127–128-74)

The war was wildly popular with the Italian people, who relished Mussolini's defiance of the League as an example of Italian greatness. Even if Mussolini had been willing to stop the war, the move would have been very unpopular in Italy.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000128-73) Kallis wrote, "Especially after the imposition of sanctions in November 1935, the popularity of the Fascist regime reached unprecedented heights".[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000128-73) On 13 December, details of the pact were leaked by a French newspaper and denounced as a sellout of the Ethiopians. The British government disassociated itself from the pact and British Foreign Secretary Sir [Samuel Hoare](/wiki/Samuel_Hoare,_1st_Viscount_Templewood "Samuel Hoare, 1st Viscount Templewood") was forced to resign in disgrace.[[74]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968187–210-75) The regime then organized a domestic mobilization known as [Oro alla Patria](/wiki/Oro_alla_Patria "Oro alla Patria"), a fundraising campaign organized on 18 December in response to the sanctions, during which Italians donated their gold to the state to support the costs of the war in Ethiopia. In [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome") alone, more than 250,000 rings were collected, while about 180,000 were gathered in [Milan](/wiki/Milan "Milan"), amid other personal gold [jewelry](/wiki/Jewelry "Jewelry") and objects totaling 57,114,395 [kilograms](/wiki/Kilogram "Kilogram") of gold and 360,618,470 of [silver](/wiki/Silver "Silver").[[75]](#cite_note-76)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/Winckel_ontmoet_Haile_Selassi.jpg/250px-Winckel_ontmoet_Haile_Selassi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Winckel_ontmoet_Haile_Selassi.jpg)

Haile Selassie with Red Cross members

In December 1935, the Ethiopian army completed its mobilization and prepared to launch the [Christmas Offensive](/wiki/Christmas_Offensive "Christmas Offensive"). The Christmas Offensive was intended to split the Italian forces in the north with the Ethiopian centre, crushing the Italian left with the Ethiopian right and to invade Eritrea with the Ethiopian left. *Ras* Seyum Mangasha held the area around [Abiy Addi](/wiki/Abiy_Addi "Abiy Addi") with about 30,000 men. *Ras* Imru was advancing from [Begemder](/wiki/Begemder "Begemder") with 40,000 men. *Ras* Kassa Haile Darge with around 40,000 men advanced from [Dessie](/wiki/Dessie "Dessie") to support *Ras* Seyoum in the centre in a push towards Warieu Pass. [*Ras* Mulugeta Yeggazu](/wiki/Mulugeta_Yeggazu "Mulugeta Yeggazu"), the Minister of War, advanced from [Dessie](/wiki/Dessie "Dessie") with approximately 80,000 men to take positions on and around [Amba Aradam](/wiki/Amba_Aradam "Amba Aradam") to the right of *Ras* Seyoum.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197145-77) The four commanders had approximately 190,000 men facing the Italians. *Ras* Imru and his "Army of the Left" were on the Ethiopian left. *Ras* Seyoum and his Army of Tigre and *Ras* Kassa and his Army of [Beghemder](/wiki/Beghemder "Beghemder") were the Ethiopian centre. *Ras* Mulugeta and his "Army of the Center" (*Mahel Sefari*) were on the Ethiopian right.[[76]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197145-77)

On 15 December, a battle took place in the region of May Timket, not far from [Tekeze River](/wiki/Tekeze_River "Tekeze River"), where the soldiers of *Ras* Imru advancing from [Begemder](/wiki/Begemder "Begemder") attacked the thin Italian defensive line garrisoned by the four battalions of Blackshirts led by General [Filippo Diamanti](/w/index.php?title=Filippo_Diamanti&action=edit&redlink=1 "Filippo Diamanti (page does not exist)"). In the following days, there were fierce battles at the Dembeguina Pass with heavy losses for both sides, the irregulars of Major Luigi Criniti were forced to retreat from [Shire](/wiki/Shire,_Ethiopia "Shire, Ethiopia") and a squadron of [L3/35](/wiki/L3/35 "L3/35") tanks led by [Ettore Crippa](/w/index.php?title=Ettore_Crippa&action=edit&redlink=1 "Ettore Crippa (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Ettore_Crippa "it:Ettore Crippa")] and [Franco Martelli](/w/index.php?title=Franco_Martelli&action=edit&redlink=1 "Franco Martelli (page does not exist)") [[it](https://it.wikipedia.org/wiki/Franco_Martelli "it:Franco Martelli")] was completely neutralized. *Ras* Imru's troops failed to widen the assault; however, the Italians also had to withdraw from large territory, withdrawing closer to their main positions at Aksum after being reinforced by the [2nd Eritrean Division](/wiki/Italian_2nd_Eritrean_Division "Italian 2nd Eritrean Division") and the [2nd CC.NN. Division](/wiki/2nd_CC.NN._Division_%2228_Ottobre%22 "2nd CC.NN. Division \"28 Ottobre\""). *Ras* Seyoum's army attempted an assault in [Tembien](/wiki/Tembien "Tembien"), at [Abiy Addi](/wiki/Abiy_Addi "Abiy Addi"), which Colonel [Ruggero Tracchia](/wiki/Ruggero_Tracchia "Ruggero Tracchia")'s men repelled thanks to their superior fire power, but they eventually had to withdraw from [Abiy Addi](/wiki/Abiy_Addi "Abiy Addi") on December 27.[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79)

The Ethiopian counteroffensive managed to stop the Italian advance for a few weeks, but the superiority of the Italian's weaponry (artillery and machine guns) as well as aerial bombardment with [chemical weapons](/wiki/Chemical_weapon "Chemical weapon"), at first with [mustard gas](/wiki/Mustard_gas "Mustard gas") prevented the Ethiopians from taking advantage of their initial successes. Having spent a decade accumulating poison gas in East Africa, Mussolini gave Badoglio authority to resort to *[Schrecklichkeit](/wiki/Schrecklichkeit "Schrecklichkeit")* (frightfulness), which included destroying villages and using gas (OC 23/06, 28 December 1935).[[79]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1983231–232-80)[[b]](#cite_note-82) The military directive had also called for a ruthless "take no prisoners" policy and the "complete destruction of the Abyssinian armies."[[81]](#cite_note-83) From 22 December to 18 January, as the Ethiopian armies recaptured southern [Tembien](/wiki/Tembien "Tembien") and were approaching [Mekelle](/wiki/Mekelle "Mekelle"), over 2,000 quintals of gas were dropped on Tigray, in particular in the [Tekeze area](/wiki/Tekeze_River "Tekeze River"): in this way, soldiers and civilians who used those waters to quench their thirst were indiscriminately hit.[[82]](#cite_note-84)

### Second Italian advance

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Second Italian advance")]

Main articles: [First Battle of Tembien](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Tembien "First Battle of Tembien"), [Battle of Amba Aradam](/wiki/Battle_of_Amba_Aradam "Battle of Amba Aradam"), [Second Battle of Tembien](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Tembien "Second Battle of Tembien"), [Battle of Shire (1936)](/wiki/Battle_of_Shire_(1936) "Battle of Shire (1936)"), and [Battle of Maychew](/wiki/Battle_of_Maychew "Battle of Maychew")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Ethiopia_War_Map_%28may_1936%29_it.svg/250px-Ethiopia_War_Map_%28may_1936%29_it.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Ethiopia_War_Map_(may_1936)_it.svg)

A map showing the military actions from February to May 1936

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c4/AO-Etiopia-1936-B-batterie-28-ottobre.jpg/250px-AO-Etiopia-1936-B-batterie-28-ottobre.jpg)](/wiki/File:AO-Etiopia-1936-B-batterie-28-ottobre.jpg)

Italian soldiers with a field gun

As the progress of the Christmas Offensive slowed, Italian plans to renew the advance on the northern front began as Mussolini had given permission to use [poison gas](/wiki/Chemical_weapon "Chemical weapon") and Badoglio received the Italian [III Corps](/wiki/3rd_Army_Corps_(Italy) "3rd Army Corps (Italy)") and the Italian [IV Corps](/wiki/COMALP "COMALP") in Eritrea during early 1936. Late in the previous year, *Ras* [Desta Damtew](/wiki/Desta_Damtew "Desta Damtew") assembled up his army in the area around [Dolo](/wiki/Dolo,_Ethiopia "Dolo, Ethiopia") to invade Italian Somaliland. Between 12 and 16 January 1936, at the [Battle of Genale Doria](/wiki/Battle_of_Genale_Doria "Battle of Genale Doria"), after defeating a small counterattack, the Italians under Marshal [Rodolfo Graziani](/wiki/Rodolfo_Graziani "Rodolfo Graziani") advanced in three columns, with air support using explosives and gas, crushing the Ethiopian forces. By 19 January, Italian vanguards entered [Negele Borana](/wiki/Negele_Borana "Negele Borana"), marking the first major Italian victory in the campaign and the near-total destruction of Desta's army.[[83]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197176-85)

In mid-January, the armies of *Ras* [Kassa Haile Darge](/wiki/Kassa_Haile_Darge "Kassa Haile Darge") and *Ras* [Seyoum Mengesha](/wiki/Seyoum_Mengesha "Seyoum Mengesha") entered [Tembien](/wiki/Tembien "Tembien") aiming to split the Italian forces at [Mekelle](/wiki/Mekelle "Mekelle"), but their plan was too ambitious for poorly supplied troops. [Pietro Badoglio](/wiki/Pietro_Badoglio "Pietro Badoglio") countered by advancing the III and II Corps to block their flanks. During the battle, disaster was narrowly avoided at the Warieu Pass when the forces of *Ras* Kassa managed to encircle the [2nd CC.NN. Division](/wiki/2nd_CC.NN._Division_%2228_Ottobre%22 "2nd CC.NN. Division \"28 Ottobre\""), but the Blackshirts held with support from air attacks using mustard gas and broke the encirclement by January 24. As a result, the [First Battle of Tembien](/wiki/First_Battle_of_Tembien "First Battle of Tembien") ended inconclusively. On 30 January 1936, Mussolini warned the Italian public that at least another year would be required to complete the conquest of Ethiopia.[[84]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968224–225-86)[[85]](#cite_note-87)

At the beginning of February, Badoglio launched an offensive against [*Ras* Mulugeta](/wiki/Mulugeta_Yeggazu "Mulugeta Yeggazu")'s forces, concentrated on [Amba Aradam](/wiki/Amba_Aradam "Amba Aradam"), numbered approximately 80,000 men. On 10 February 1936, General [Ruggero Santini](/wiki/Ruggero_Santini "Ruggero Santini")'s I Corps and General [Ettore Bastico](/wiki/Ettore_Bastico "Ettore Bastico")'s III Corps began their encirclement of the Amba Aradam massif under a heavy thunderstorm. Ras Mulugeta's army did not appear until 12 February, when it engaged the Blackshirts of the [4th CC.NN. Division](/wiki/4th_CC.NN._Division_%223_Gennaio%22 "4th CC.NN. Division \"3 Gennaio\"") under General Alessandro Traditi on the left. On the right, the Ethiopians attacked the III Corps, seriously engaging General Francesco Bertini's [27th Infantry Division](/wiki/27th_Infantry_Division_%22Brescia%22 "27th Infantry Division \"Brescia\"") on the Dansa-Bet Quinet slopes. Between 13 and 14 February, Italian forces consolidated their positions and prepared for the final assault, while Ethiopian forces were barely able to mount a counterattack. Badoglio's forces, clearly superior, advanced systematically, and Mulugeta ordered a retreat on 15 February. By the evening, Italian forces rejoined behind Amba Aradam as Ethiopian troops withdrew chaotically toward [Amba Alagi](/wiki/Amba_Alagi "Amba Alagi") and [Shire](/wiki/Shire,_Ethiopia "Shire, Ethiopia"). On 16 February, Badoglio ordered air attacks on the remnants of Mulugeta's army, which continued until 19 February, concluding with the capture of Amba Aradam and the dissolution of Mulugeta's forces. *Ras* Mulugeta and his son were killed on 26 February by a band of [Azebo Oromo](/wiki/Azebo_Oromo "Azebo Oromo") brigands that slaughtered Ethiopian stranglers struggling to return home.[[86]](#cite_note-88)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b1/Campagna_di_Etiopia_-_Battaglia_dell%27Amba_Aradam_-_01.jpg/250px-Campagna_di_Etiopia_-_Battaglia_dell%27Amba_Aradam_-_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Campagna_di_Etiopia_-_Battaglia_dell%27Amba_Aradam_-_01.jpg)

The 3rd Battalion of the [27th Infantry Division](/wiki/27th_Infantry_Division_%22Brescia%22 "27th Infantry Division \"Brescia\"") during the [Battle of Amba Aradam](/wiki/Battle_of_Amba_Aradam "Battle of Amba Aradam"), February 1936

As the attack on [Amba Aradam](/wiki/Amba_Aradam "Amba Aradam") ended, [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") left [Dessie](/wiki/Dessie "Dessie") on 20 February to regroup scattered forces and link up with *Ras* [Kassa Haile Darge](/wiki/Kassa_Haile_Darge "Kassa Haile Darge") and *Ras* [Seyoum Mengesha](/wiki/Seyoum_Mengesha "Seyoum Mengesha") retreating from [Tembien](/wiki/Tembien "Tembien") to [Amba Alagi](/wiki/Amba_Alagi "Amba Alagi"). Meanwhile, Badoglio launched a pincer attack on the armies of Kassa, Seyoum, and Imru. Amid confusion and indecision in the Ethiopian command, Italian forces under [Ettore Bastico](/wiki/Ettore_Bastico "Ettore Bastico") and [Alessandro Pirzio Biroli](/wiki/Alessandro_Pirzio_Biroli "Alessandro Pirzio Biroli") advanced rapidly, capturing key positions such as [Amba Alagi](/wiki/Amba_Alagi "Amba Alagi") in a attempt to surround *Ras* Kassa's army near [Abiy Addi](/wiki/Abiy_Addi "Abiy Addi"). On 29 February, *Ras* Kassa ordered a general retreat, but it was too late and about a third of his army found itself surrounded while the remainder was unable to stay united as they chaotically retreated, marking the end of the [Second Battle of Tembien](/wiki/Second_Battle_of_Tembien "Second Battle of Tembien").[[87]](#cite_note-89)

From 28 February to early March, the Italian II and IV Corps moved against *Ras* Imru in [Shire](/wiki/Shire,_Ethiopia "Shire, Ethiopia"), facing strong resistance at [Selekleka](/wiki/Selekleka "Selekleka") and the Af Gagà pass but ultimately forcing a retreat. Badoglio, seeing Ras Imru's army slip away, immediately ordered the air force to employ all available means to hunt down the enemy forces. Between March 3 and 4, one hundred and twenty fighters and bombers bombarded the Ethiopian forces as they crossed the [Tekezé River](/wiki/Tekez%C3%A9_River "Tekezé River"), with 63,600 kilograms of explosives, mustard gas, and incendiary bombs, decimating Imru's troops, who suffered between 3,000 and 5,000 casualties among his army. By 3 March, Haile Selassie reached [Korem](/wiki/Korem "Korem"), trying to muster forces. There, he learned that Mulugeta and his sons were dead, Amba Alagi had fallen to the Italians, the [Azebo Galla](/wiki/Azebo_Galla "Azebo Galla") revolted, and desertions plagued his troops.[[88]](#cite_note-90)

After a series of Italian victories in Tigray, the war turned decisively in Italy's favor. While Ethiopian forces retreated in disarray, the I Corps and the Eritrean Corps advanced toward the [Maychew](/wiki/Maychew "Maychew") basin to confront [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie"). The emperor could muster only about 30,000–35,000 men and chose to make a decisive stand, in keeping with Ethiopian tradition that required the monarch to lead in the final battle. He scheduled the attack for 31 March, also influenced by reports that Italian forces at Maychew were still limited in number. On 20 March, Haile Selassie moved from [Korem](/wiki/Korem "Korem") to Amba Aia, where he met his commanders to plan the offensive. By the time the Ethiopians advanced, about 31,000 troops in three columns faced roughly 40,000 Italian and colonial forces under Generals [Ruggero Santini](/wiki/Ruggero_Santini "Ruggero Santini") and [Alessandro Pirzio Biroli](/wiki/Alessandro_Pirzio_Biroli "Alessandro Pirzio Biroli"), with superior firepower and air support. These delays proved decisive, enabling the Italians to reinforce the front, continue aerial bombardment, and gather intelligence on Ethiopian movements.[[89]](#cite_note-91)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Italian_artillery_during_the_Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War.jpg/250px-Italian_artillery_during_the_Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italian_artillery_during_the_Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War.jpg)

Italian artillery operated by [Somali Ascari](/wiki/Italian_Somaliland "Italian Somaliland") troops

On 31 March 1936, the final Ethiopian offensive ordered by [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") began, marking the beginning of the [Battle of Maychew](/wiki/Battle_of_Maychew "Battle of Maychew"). Ethiopian troops launched repeated assaults, aiming to break the Italian lines. Despite initial gains, the [Italian 1st Army Corps](/wiki/I_Army_Corps_(Italy) "I Army Corps (Italy)"), supported by artillery and a decisive intervention from the *[Regia Aeronautica](/wiki/Regia_Aeronautica "Regia Aeronautica")*, halted their advance. By midday, the Italian [Alpini](/wiki/Alpini "Alpini") and Eritrean [askari](/wiki/Askari "Askari") units launched counterattacks, recapturing lost ground after intense fighting. On 1 April, after a final Ethiopian attack was easily repulsed, [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") ordered his remaining 20,000 demoralized and disorganized men to retreat towards the heights of [Korem](/wiki/Korem "Korem"). Badoglio, having learned that the Ethiopians were retreating, ordered the *Regia Aeronautica* to strike the retreating Ethiopians on the basin of [Lake Ashenge](/wiki/Lake_Ashenge "Lake Ashenge"), who were continually harassed by [Azebo Galla](/wiki/Azebo_Galla "Azebo Galla") irregulars and the artillery of the [1st Eritrean Division](/wiki/Italian_1st_Eritrean_Division "Italian 1st Eritrean Division") under General [Gustavo Pesenti](/wiki/Gustavo_Pesenti "Gustavo Pesenti"). On April 4th, the Air Bombing Brigade under General [Vincenzo Magliocco](/wiki/Vincenzo_Magliocco "Vincenzo Magliocco") dropped 700 quintals of bombs (including mustard gas) on the routing Ethiopians, who were completely exposed and without air cover, resulting in the massacre of thousands of Ethiopian soldiers.[[90]](#cite_note-92)[[91]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968247–256-93)

The defeat at Maychew led to the complete dissolution of the Ethiopian army on the northern front as the demoralized troops began to desert in large numbers, which cleared the way for the Italian troops.[[92]](#cite_note-94) Taking advantage of the collapse of the Ethiopian armies, the East African Fast Column (Colonna Celere dell'Africa Orientale) of General [Achille Starace](/wiki/Achille_Starace "Achille Starace"), composed of some 3,000 troops on more than 400 vehicles occupied [Gondar](/wiki/Gondar "Gondar") on 1 April, and soon took the region around [Lake Tana](/wiki/Lake_Tana "Lake Tana"). Additionally, an Italian column of 17,000 men deployed from [Assab](/wiki/Assab "Assab") captured [Aussa](/wiki/Asaita "Asaita") on the same day, a town that controlled one of the main caravan routes to Addis Ababa.[[93]](#cite_note-95) After a lull in the Southern front, on 14 April, Graziani launched an offensive against *Ras* [Nasibu Emmanual](/wiki/Nasibu_Emmanual "Nasibu Emmanual") to defeat the last Ethiopian army in the field at the [Battle of the Ogaden](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Ogaden "Battle of the Ogaden"). The Ethiopians were drawn up behind a defensive line that was termed the "Hindenburg Wall", designed by the chief of staff of Nasibu, and [Wehib Pasha](/wiki/Wehib_Pasha "Wehib Pasha"), a seasoned ex-Ottoman commander. Despite strong Ethiopian resistance and heavy losses for the Italians, Graziani's forces, supported by air power, broke through the defenses by 25 April and forced an Ethiopian retreat; 2,000 Italian soldiers and 5,000 Ethiopian soldiers were killed or wounded.[[94]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971121,_123-96)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f5/Column_of_Italian_colonial_troops_in_Ethiopia.jpg/250px-Column_of_Italian_colonial_troops_in_Ethiopia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Column_of_Italian_colonial_troops_in_Ethiopia.jpg)

Column of Italian colonial troops and vehicles in Ethiopia

### Fall of Addis Ababa

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Fall of Addis Ababa")]

Main article: [March of the Iron Will](/wiki/March_of_the_Iron_Will "March of the Iron Will")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/Italian_troops_marching_in_Ethiopia.jpg/250px-Italian_troops_marching_in_Ethiopia.jpg)](/wiki/File:Italian_troops_marching_in_Ethiopia.jpg)

Column of Italian troops marching

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0c/AO-Etiopia-1936-H-Cavalleria-indigena-verso-Addis-Abeba.jpg/250px-AO-Etiopia-1936-H-Cavalleria-indigena-verso-Addis-Abeba.jpg)](/wiki/File:AO-Etiopia-1936-H-Cavalleria-indigena-verso-Addis-Abeba.jpg)

Italian colonial troops advance on Addis Ababa

On 7 April 1936, an increasingly desperate [Haile Selassie](/wiki/Haile_Selassie "Haile Selassie") issued a proclamation that called to arms all Ethiopian males capable of military service. However, most of those who responded lacked military training and, at best, had antiquated weapons. This disparity was coupled with vicious Italian tactics like mass executions of Ethiopian soldiers and civilians and the use of chemical weapons. On 26 April 1936, Badoglio began the "March of the Iron Will" from Dessie to Addis Ababa, an advance with a mechanised column against slight Ethiopian resistance.[[95]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971109-97)[[96]](#cite_note-98)

Meanwhile, Selassie conducted a disorganized retreat towards the capital. There, government officials operated without leadership, unable to contact the Emperor and unsure of his whereabouts.[[97]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpencer200658–59-99) Realizing that Addis Ababa would soon fall to the Italians, Ethiopian administrators met to discuss a possible evacuation of the government to the west. After several days, they decided that they should relocate to [Gore](/wiki/Gore,_Ethiopia "Gore, Ethiopia"), though actual preparations for their departure were postponed.[[98]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpencer200659-100) Addis Ababa became crowded with retreating soldiers from the front while its foreign residents sought refuge at various European legations.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpencer200661-101) Selassie reached the capital on 30 April. That day his [Council of Ministers](/wiki/Council_of_Ministers_(Ethiopia) "Council of Ministers (Ethiopia)") resolved that the city should be defended and a retreat to Gore conducted only as a last resort.[[99]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpencer200661-101)

The following day a council of Ethiopian nobles convened to re-examine the decision, where Ras [Aberra Kassa](/wiki/Aberra_Kassa "Aberra Kassa") suggested that the Emperor should go to Geneva to appeal to the League of Nations for assistance before returning to lead resistance against the Italians. The view was subsequently adopted by Selassie and preparations were made for his departure.[[100]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESpencer200662-102) On 2 May, Selassie boarded a train from [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa") to [Djibouti](/wiki/Djibouti "Djibouti"), with the gold of the Ethiopian Central Bank. From there he fled to the United Kingdom, with the tacit acquiescence of the Italians who could have bombed his train, into exile.[[101]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971126-103)

Before he departed, Selassie ordered that the government of Ethiopia be moved to Gore and directed the mayor of Addis Ababa to maintain order in the city until the Italians' arrival. [Imru Haile Selassie](/wiki/Imru_Haile_Selassie "Imru Haile Selassie") was appointed [Prince Regent](/wiki/Regent "Regent") during his absence. The city police, under [Abebe Aregai](/wiki/Abebe_Aregai "Abebe Aregai") and the remainder of the Imperial Guard did their utmost to restrain a growing crowd but rioters rampaged throughout the city, looting and setting fire to shops owned by Europeans. Most of the violence occurred between looters, fighting over the spoils and by 5 May, much of the city lay in ruins.[[102]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971125-104) At 04:00 Badoglio drove into the city at the head of 1,600 lorries and patrols of Italian tanks, troops and Carabinieri were sent to occupy tactically valuable areas in the city, as the remaining inhabitants watched sullenly.[[103]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968262–263-105)

The news of the fall of Addis Ababa was celebrated in Italy. On the evening of 9 May 1936, after sirens had called the population to assemble throughout the country, Mussolini appeared on the balcony of [Palazzo Venezia](/wiki/Palazzo_Venezia "Palazzo Venezia") and delivered the proclamation of the [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire") to a large crowd of 200,000 people. During the speech, Mussolini stated that "the destiny of Ethiopia has been sealed today" and "Italy at last has her empire".[[104]](#cite_note-106) On the same day, the [Grand Council of Fascism](/wiki/Grand_Council_of_Fascism "Grand Council of Fascism") recognized Mussolini with the title of "Founder of the Empire," which [Achille Starace](/wiki/Achille_Starace "Achille Starace") promptly incorporated into the official formula of the "salute to the Duce." From that moment, and for many months afterward, the press and leading political and cultural figures engaged in continuous glorification of Mussolini's image, to the point that General [Enrico Caviglia](/wiki/Enrico_Caviglia "Enrico Caviglia") observed that Mussolini was drawn into a climate of flattering rhetoric "which dangerously accentuated the already extreme confidence he had in his own political ability."[[105]](#cite_note-107)

### Subsequent operations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Subsequent operations")]

Main article: [Arbegnoch](/wiki/Arbegnoch "Arbegnoch")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8f/Military_Parade_of_Italian_Troops_in_Addis_Ababa_%281936%29.jpg/250px-Military_Parade_of_Italian_Troops_in_Addis_Ababa_%281936%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Military_Parade_of_Italian_Troops_in_Addis_Ababa_(1936).jpg)

Italian troops in Addis Ababa, 1936

Despite the capture of Addis Ababa and the proclamation of the Empire, Italian troops were facing serious difficulties. The rainy season hindered operations and the vast arid regions of south-western Ethiopia, almost devoid of communication routes, had not yet been occupied by the Italians. In this vast territory the remnants of the Ethiopian army were still active, consisting of approximately 40,000–50,000 men. On 10 May 1936, Italian troops from the northern front and from the southern front met at [Dire Dawa](/wiki/Dire_Dawa "Dire Dawa").[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicolle199712-108) The Italians found the recently released Ethiopian Ras, [Hailu Tekle Haymanot](/wiki/Hailu_Tekle_Haymanot "Hailu Tekle Haymanot"), who boarded a train back to Addis Ababa and approached the Italian invaders in submission.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971127-109) The remnants of the Ethiopian army under Imru Haile Selassie fell back to [Gore](/wiki/Gore,_Ethiopia "Gore, Ethiopia") in western Ethiopia to reorganise a new provisional government and continue the resistance against the Italians.[[108]](#cite_note-110) The exiled government in Gore was never able to provide any meaningful leadership to the remaining military formations but sporadic resistance by independent groups persisted around the capital.[[109]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbinkDe_BruijnVan_Walraven200397-111)

On 1 June 1936, the Italian government officially unified the three territories of Ethiopia, Eritrea, and Somalia into a single legal and political entity designated [Italian East Africa](/wiki/Italian_East_Africa "Italian East Africa"), Badoglio became the first [Viceroy](/wiki/Viceroy "Viceroy") of Italian East Africa but on 11 June, he was replaced by Marshal Graziani.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcus199932-112) Marshal Graziani sent a small air expedition to [Nekemte](/wiki/Nekemte "Nekemte") to convince an Oromo chief to submit to the Italian forces in order to establish an Italian garrison in the western region, which would facilitate the conquest of western Ethiopia. However, on the night of 26–27 June, [Ethiopian guerrillas](/wiki/Arbegnoch "Arbegnoch") attacked the expedition during the [Lechemti Massacre](/wiki/Lechemti_Massacre "Lechemti Massacre") after it landed in [Nekemte](/wiki/Nekemte "Nekemte"), destroying three Italian aircraft and killing twelve Italian officials, including Air Marshal [Vincenzo Magliocco](/wiki/Vincenzo_Magliocco "Vincenzo Magliocco") (the deputy viceroy of [Italian East Africa](/wiki/Italian_East_Africa "Italian East Africa")) and aviator [Antonio Locatelli](/wiki/Antonio_Locatelli "Antonio Locatelli"). Graziani ordered the town to be bombed in retaliation for the killings.[[111]](#cite_note-113)[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAbbinkDe_BruijnVan_Walraven2003102-114)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Massacre_de_L%C3%A9kemti.jpg/250px-Massacre_de_L%C3%A9kemti.jpg)](/wiki/File:Massacre_de_L%C3%A9kemti.jpg)

Illustration of the [Lechemti Massacre](/wiki/Lechemti_Massacre "Lechemti Massacre")

The situation in [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa") in the first months after its capture was difficult for the Italians. Communications were possible only via the long route from [Somalia](/wiki/Italian_Somaliland "Italian Somaliland"), while violence and disorder were widespread in the city. Marshal [Rodolfo Graziani](/wiki/Rodolfo_Graziani "Rodolfo Graziani"), initially with only 9,000 troops at his disposal, feared an attack by Ethiopian guerrillas, reported to be "all around Addis Ababa," and rumors circulated that thousands of [Ethiopian guerrillas](/wiki/Arbegnoch "Arbegnoch") were preparing to strike.[[113]](#cite_note-115) On 28 July, remnants of the Ethiopian army under the brothers [Wondosson](/wiki/Wondosson_Kassa "Wondosson Kassa"), [Asfawossen](/wiki/Asfawossen_Kassa "Asfawossen Kassa") and [Aberra Kassa](/wiki/Aberra_Kassa "Aberra Kassa") supported by the irregulars of [Abebe Aregai](/wiki/Abebe_Aregai "Abebe Aregai") and [Mesfin Sileshi](/wiki/Mesfin_Sileshi "Mesfin Sileshi") attacked Addis Ababa. The Ethiopians advanced into the centre of Addis Ababa where they unleashed panic, however the [30th Infantry Division "Sabauda"](/wiki/30th_Infantry_Division_%22Sabauda%22 "30th Infantry Division \"Sabauda\"") under General [Italo Gariboldi](/wiki/Italo_Gariboldi "Italo Gariboldi") and the 1st Eritrean Mixed Brigade under General [Sebastiano Gallina](/wiki/Sebastiano_Gallina "Sebastiano Gallina") eventually forced them to retreat on 30 July.[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcus199932-112)

After the end of the rainy season, an Italian column led by [Carlo Geloso](/wiki/Carlo_Geloso "Carlo Geloso") left Addis Ababa in September and occupied most of western Ethiopia a month later, including Gore and Nekemte. The forces of *Ras* Imru were trapped between the Italians and the [Gojeb River](/wiki/Gojeb_River "Gojeb River") and Imru surrendered on 19 December. That same day, [Wondosson Kassa](/wiki/Wondosson_Kassa "Wondosson Kassa") was captured and executed near [Debre Zebit](/wiki/Debre_Zebit "Debre Zebit") and on 21 December, [Aberra Kassa](/wiki/Aberra_Kassa "Aberra Kassa") and [Asfawossen Kassa](/wiki/Asfawossen_Kassa "Asfawossen Kassa") were executed in [Fikke](/wiki/Fiche,_Ethiopia "Fiche, Ethiopia").[[110]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcus199932-112) Imru was flown to Italy and imprisoned on the Island of [Ponza](/wiki/Ponza "Ponza"), while the rest of the [Ethiopian prisoners](/wiki/Ethiopian_POWs_during_the_Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War "Ethiopian POWs during the Second Italo-Ethiopian War") taken in the war were dispersed in camps in East Africa and Italy. A second column went south-west to attack the last remnants of the old Ethiopian army under *Ras* [Desta Damtew](/wiki/Desta_Damtew "Desta Damtew") who had assembled forces in the Great Lakes district. The Ethiopians were defeated on 16 December and by January, the Italians had established a measure of control over the provinces of [Sidamo](/wiki/Sidamo_Province "Sidamo Province"), [Kaffa](/wiki/Kaffa_Province "Kaffa Province") and [Arsi](/wiki/Arsi_Province "Arsi Province"). After another two months, the remaining Ethiopians were surrounded and fought on, rather than surrender.[[114]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968282-116) On 19 February 1937 the last battle of the war occurred when remnants of the old Ethiopian army under *Ras* Desta clashed with Italian forces at Gogetti, and were defeated. An estimated 4,000 Ethiopians were reportedly killed in the engagement, 1,600 of whom—including Damtew—after being taken prisoner.[[2]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMockler2003172–173-2)

### Casualties

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Casualties")]

In 1968, Colonel A. J. Barker wrote that from 1 January 1935 to 31 May 1936, the Italian army and Blackshirt units lost 1,148 men killed, 125 men died of wounds and thirty-one missing; about 1,593 Eritrean troops and 453 civilian workmen were also killed, a total of 3,319 casualties.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968292–293-19) In a 1978 publication, Alberto Sbacchi wrote that these official Italian casualty figures of about 3,000 were an underestimate.[[115]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESbacchi197837-117) Sbacchi wrote that the official total of Italian casualties was unreliable, because the regime desired to underestimate Italian losses.[[116]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESbacchi1997xxi-118) [Angelo Del Boca](/wiki/Angelo_Del_Boca "Angelo Del Boca") estimates the total Italian losses up to 31 December 1936 speak of 2,317 dead for the Italian army, 1,165 for the [Blackshirts](/wiki/Blackshirts "Blackshirts"), 193 from the air force, 56 from the navy, 78 civilians in the Gondrand shipyard massacre, 453 factory workers and 88 merchant marines, for a total of 4,350 Italians killed.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDel_Boca2009717–719-10) To these figures must be added approximately 9,000 injured and 18,200 repatriated due to illness.[[10]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTERochat2008128-11) Estimates on the losses of the askaris, however very vague, he puts it at 4,500 killed.[[9]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDel_Boca2009717–719-10) From 1936 to 1940, there was an additional 9,555 men killed and 144,000 sick and wounded.[[117]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESbacchi197836–38,_43-119) Total Italian casualties from 1935 to 1940 according to these calculations were about 208,000 killed or wounded. Based on 1,911 Italians killed in the first six months of 1940, Ministry of Africa figures for 6 May 1936 to 10 June 1940 are 8,284 men killed, which Sbacchi considered to be fairly accurate.[[25]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESbacchi197843-26)

There was a lack of reliable statistics because confusion during the invasion made it difficult to keep accurate records and the *Statistical Bulletin* had ceased to provide data on fatalities. Field hospital records had been destroyed, inventories dispersed, individual deaths were not reported and bodies were not repatriated to Italy. Unpublished reports listed 3,694 military and civilian fatalities among 44,000 casualties and from May 1936 to June 1940, there were another 12,248 military and civilian fatalities in 144,000 casualties.[[118]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESbacchi1997xxi–xxii-120) The Italian estimation of Ethiopian losses are 50,000 men killed in the Northern front and 20,000 men killed in the Southern front for a total of 70,000 battle deaths. Conversely, in a memorandum submitted to the Paris conference in 1946, the Ethiopian government enumerated 275,000 men killed in action, 78,500 Patriots killed in hostilities during the occupation from 1936 to 1941, 17,800 women and children killed by bombing, 30,000 people killed in the massacre of February 1937, 35,000 people died in concentration camps, 24,000 Patriots killed in obedience to orders from summary courts, 300,000 people died after their villages had been destroyed, a total of 760,300 deaths.[[119]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker196891-121) However, Del Boca claims that these figures are unreliable and were likely exaggerated to extract more reparations. He asserts that the Italian estimation is more accurate.[[120]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEDel_Boca2009717–720-122)

## War crimes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: War crimes")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d2/24._Adolf_Hoffmeister%2C_%C5%98%C3%ADm_nese_%C4%8Dernou_kulturu_Habe%C5%A1an%C5%AFm%2C_1936.jpg/250px-24._Adolf_Hoffmeister%2C_%C5%98%C3%ADm_nese_%C4%8Dernou_kulturu_Habe%C5%A1an%C5%AFm%2C_1936.jpg)](/wiki/File:24._Adolf_Hoffmeister,_%C5%98%C3%ADm_nese_%C4%8Dernou_kulturu_Habe%C5%A1an%C5%AFm,_1936.jpg)

*Rome brings black culture to the Abbysians*, caricature by [Adolf Hoffmeister](/wiki/Adolf_Hoffmeister "Adolf Hoffmeister"), 1936

### Use of chemical weapons

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Use of chemical weapons")]

Italian military forces used between 300 and 500 tons of mustard gas to attack both military and civilian targets,[[121]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1983231-123) despite being a signatory to the 1925 [Geneva Protocol](/wiki/Geneva_Protocol "Geneva Protocol") banning the practice. This gas had been produced during [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") and subsequently transported to East Africa. [J. F. C. Fuller](/wiki/J._F._C._Fuller "J. F. C. Fuller"), who was present in Ethiopia during the conflict, stated that mustard gas "was the decisive tactical factor in the war."[[122]](#cite_note-Legro-124) Historian [Walter Laqueur](/wiki/Walter_Laqueur "Walter Laqueur") estimates that up to one-third of Ethiopian casualties of the war were caused by chemical weapons.[[123]](#cite_note-125)

The Italians claimed that their use of gas was justified by the execution of [Tito Minniti](/wiki/Tito_Minniti "Tito Minniti") and his observer in Ogaden by Ethiopian forces.[[124]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaudendistel2006131-126) However, the use of gas was authorized by Mussolini nearly two months before Minniti's death on 26 December 1935, as evidenced by the following order:

> Rome, October 27, 1935. To His Excellency Graziani. The use of gas as an *ultima ratio* to overwhelm enemy resistance and in case of counter-attack is authorized. Mussolini.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1983231,_417-23)

After Minniti's death, the order was expanded to use of gas "on a vast scale":

> Rome, December 28, 1935. To His Excellency Badoglio. Given the enemy system I have authorized Your Excellency the use even on a vast scale of any gas and flamethrowers. Mussolini.[[22]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1983231,_417-23)

Military and civilian targets were gas bombed and on 30 December, a [Red Cross](/wiki/International_Committee_of_the_Red_Cross "International Committee of the Red Cross") unit was bombed at Dolo and an Egyptian ambulance was attacked at Bulale; a few days later an Egyptian medical unit was bombed at Daggah Bur. There were more attacks in January and February, then on 4 March 1936, a British Red Cross camp near Quoram appeared to be subject to the most deliberate attack of all, when low-flying Italian aircraft crews could not have missed the big Red Cross signs.[[58]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968222-59) Mustard gas was also sprayed from above on Ethiopian combatants and villages. The Italians tried to keep their resort to chemical warfare secret but were exposed by the International Red Cross and many foreign observers. The Italians claimed that at least 19 bombardments of Red Cross tents "posted in the areas of military encampment of the Ethiopian resistance", had been "erroneous".[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The Italians delivered poison gas by [gas shell](/wiki/Shell_(projectile) "Shell (projectile)") and in bombs dropped by the *Regia Aeronautica*. Though poorly equipped, the Ethiopians had achieved some success against modern weaponry but had no defence against the "terrible rain that burned and killed".[[125]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker197156-127)

In general, historians concluded that the use of chemical weapons was effective and devastating to morale and manpower, though some disagree and argue that they had negligible effect in battle.[[126]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEClark195920-128) Historian [Angelo Del Boca](/wiki/Angelo_Del_Boca "Angelo Del Boca") condemned the use of gas, but argued that it had only a minimal effect on Italian war aims.[[127]](#cite_note-129) An analysis by the [Stockholm International Peace Research Institute](/wiki/Stockholm_International_Peace_Research_Institute "Stockholm International Peace Research Institute") argued that the use of chemical weapons shifted the war in favor of the Italians, and dealt a major blow to Ethiopian morale.[[128]](#cite_note-130)

American and British military analysis came to similar conclusions. The US military concluded that "Chemical weapons were devastating against the unprepared and unprotected Ethiopians."[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmart19971–78-131) British Major General J. F. C. Fuller, assigned to the Italian army, noted that "In place of the laborious process of picketing the heights, the heights sprayed with gas were rendered unoccupiable by the enemy, save at the gravest risk. It was an exceedingly cunning use of this chemical."[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmart19971–78-131)

Haile Selassie in his report to the League of Nations described it:

> ....Special sprayers were installed on board aircraft so they could vaporize over vast areas of territory a fine, death-dealing rain. Groups of 9, 15, or 18 aircraft followed one another so that the fog issuing from them formed a continuous sheet. It was thus that, as from the end of January 1936, soldiers, women, children, cattle, rivers, lakes, and pastures were drenched continually with this deadly rain. In order more surely to poison the waters and pastures, the Italian command made its aircraft pass over and over again. These fearful tactics succeeded. Men and animals succumbed. The deadly rain that fell from the aircraft made all those whom it touched fly shrieking with pain. All those who drank poisoned water or ate infected food also succumbed in dreadful suffering. In tens of thousands the victims of Italian mustard gas fell.

— Smart[[129]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESmart19971–78-131)

### Dum-dum rounds and atrocities against prisoners of war

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Dum-dum rounds and atrocities against prisoners of war")]

Ethiopian troops used [dum-dum bullets](/wiki/Expanding_bullet "Expanding bullet"), which had been banned by declaration IV, 3 of the [Hague Convention (1899)](/wiki/Hague_Conventions_of_1899_and_1907 "Hague Conventions of 1899 and 1907") and began mutilating captured [Eritrean Askari](/wiki/Eritrean_Ascari "Eritrean Ascari") (often with [castration](/wiki/Castration "Castration")) beginning in the first weeks of war.[[26]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEAntonicelli197579-27) Some hundreds of colonial Eritrean Ascari and dozens of Italians suffered these amputations, often done before death as allegedly happened to 17 Italian workers emasculated in [Gondrand](/wiki/Gondrand_massacre "Gondrand massacre") in February 1936.[[130]](#cite_note-132)

During the first months of the invasion, Italian forces adopted an unofficial policy of "take no prisoners" and frequently executed surrendering enemy combatants, including their commanders. In May 1936, Mussolini issued an order to Graziani stating that "all rebels taken prisoner must be shot," which he followed up weeks later with further instructions to commit mass murder of "rebels" by saying "I repeat my authorization to Your Excellency to initiate and systematically conduct a policy of terror and extermination."[[131]](#cite_note-133)

### Addis Ababa massacre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Addis Ababa massacre")]

Main article: [Yekatit 12](/wiki/Yekatit_12 "Yekatit 12")

That same date, 19 February 1937 – Yekatit 12 according to the [Ge'ez calendar](/wiki/Ge%27ez_calendar "Ge'ez calendar") – saw the attempted assassination of Marshal Graziani by Eritrean rebels [Abraham Deboch](/w/index.php?title=Abraham_Deboch&action=edit&redlink=1 "Abraham Deboch (page does not exist)") and [Mogos Asgedom](/w/index.php?title=Mogos_Asgedom&action=edit&redlink=1 "Mogos Asgedom (page does not exist)") in [Addis Ababa](/wiki/Addis_Ababa "Addis Ababa"). The campaign of reprisals visited by the Italians upon the population of Addis Ababa has been described as the worst massacre in Ethiopian history.[[132]](#cite_note-134) Estimates vary on the number of people killed in the three days that followed the attempt on Graziani's life. Ethiopian sources estimated that 30,000 people were killed by the Italians, while Italian sources claimed that only a few hundred were killed. A 2017 history of the massacre estimated that 19,200 people were killed, 20 percent of the population of Addis Ababa.[[17]](#cite_note-:0-18) Over the following week, numerous Ethiopians suspected of opposing Italian rule were rounded up and executed, including members of the [Black Lions](/wiki/Black_Lions "Black Lions") and other members of the aristocracy. Many more were imprisoned, even collaborators such as *Ras* Gebre Haywot, the son of *Ras* [Mikael of Wollo](/wiki/Mikael_of_Wollo "Mikael of Wollo"), Brehane Markos, and Ayale Gebre, who had helped the Italians identify the two men who made the attempt on Graziani's life.[[133]](#cite_note-135)

According to Mockler, "Italian *[carabinieri](/wiki/Carabinieri "Carabinieri")* had fired into the crowds of beggars and poor assembled for the distribution of alms; and it is said that the Federal Secretary, Guido Cortese, even fired his revolver into the group of Ethiopian dignitaries standing around him."[[134]](#cite_note-Mockler_20032-136) Hours later, Cortese gave the fatal order:

> Comrades, today is the day when we should show our devotion to our Viceroy by reacting and destroying the Ethiopians for three days. For three days I give you ''carte blanche'' to destroy and kill and do what you want to the Ethiopians.[[134]](#cite_note-Mockler_20032-136)

Italians doused native houses with [petrol](/wiki/Petrol "Petrol") and set them on fire. They broke into the homes of local [Greeks](/wiki/Greek_people "Greek people") and [Armenians](/wiki/Armenian_People "Armenian People") and lynched their servants. Some even posed on the corpses of their victims to have their photographs taken.[[134]](#cite_note-Mockler_20032-136) The first day of the massacre has been commemorated as "[Yekatit 12](/wiki/Yekatit_12 "Yekatit 12")" (19 February) by Ethiopians ever since. There is a [Yekatit 12 monument](/wiki/Yekatit_12_monument "Yekatit 12 monument") in Addis Ababa in memory of these Ethiopian victims of Italian aggression.

## Public and international reaction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Public and international reaction")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/Haile_Selassie_Time_cover_1936.jpg/250px-Haile_Selassie_Time_cover_1936.jpg)](/wiki/File:Haile_Selassie_Time_cover_1936.jpg)

Haile Selassie's resistance to the Italian invasion made him [Time Man of the Year 1935](/wiki/Time_Person_of_the_Year "Time Person of the Year").

Italy's military victory overshadowed concerns about the economy.[[135]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMack_Smith1983234-137)[[136]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971131-138) Mussolini was at the height of his popularity in May 1936 with the proclamation of the Italian empire.[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000128-73) His biographer, [Renzo De Felice](/wiki/Renzo_De_Felice "Renzo De Felice"), called the war "Mussolini's masterpiece" as for a brief moment he had been able to create something resembling a national consensus both in favor of himself and his regime.[[137]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis2000124,_197–198-139) When Badoglio returned to Italy, he received a snub, as Mussolini made certain that the honours bestowed on Badoglio received fell short of those granted to an Italian "national hero", in order to present the victory as an achievement of the Fascist system rather than as an achievement of the traditional Italian elites, of which Badoglio was a member.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis200074-140) A sign of Mussolini's increased power and popularity after the war was his creation of a new military rank; First Marshal of the Italian Empire, to which he promoted both himself and King Victor Emmanuel III, thus putting the prime minister on a theoretical level of equality with the king.[[138]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEKallis200074-140)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/34/SelassieInJerusalem.jpg/250px-SelassieInJerusalem.jpg)](/wiki/File:SelassieInJerusalem.jpg)

Haile Selassie passes through [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem") on his way to exile in Britain.

Haile Selassie sailed from Djibouti in the British cruiser [HMS *Enterprise*](/wiki/HMS_Enterprise_(D52) "HMS Enterprise (D52)"). From [Mandatory Palestine](/wiki/Mandatory_Palestine "Mandatory Palestine") Selassie sailed to [Gibraltar](/wiki/Gibraltar "Gibraltar") en route to Britain. While still in [Jerusalem](/wiki/Jerusalem "Jerusalem"), Haile Selassie sent a [telegram](/wiki/Telegram "Telegram") to the League of Nations:

> We have decided to bring to an end the most unequal, most unjust, most barbarous war of our age, and have chosen the road to exile in order that our people will not be exterminated and in order to consecrate ourselves wholly and in peace to the preservation of our empire's independence... we now demand that the League of Nations should continue its efforts to secure respect for the covenant, and that it should decide not to recognize territorial extensions, or the exercise of an assumed sovereignty, resulting from the illegal recourse to armed force and to numerous other violations of international agreements.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971131–132-141)

The Ethiopian Emperor's telegram caused several nations to temporarily defer recognition of the Italian conquest.[[139]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971131–132-141)

How the Italian public felt about being against or for the war has a degree of uncertanty surrounding it as the general public did not have freedom of expression and censorship was practiced. Punishments for speaking out against the war varied. No sizeable resistance movement developed in Italy toward the war although a number of acts of resistance did occur with several mutinies and acts of sabotage happening. Initially the public was concerned about the risk of the British intervening in the conflict.[[140]](#cite_note-142)

On 30 June, Selassie spoke at the League of Nations and was introduced by the [President of the Assembly](/wiki/Leaders_of_the_League_of_Nations "Leaders of the League of Nations") as "His Imperial Majesty, the Emperor of Ethiopia" ("*Sa Majesté Imperiale, l'Empereur d'Ethiopie*"). A group of jeering Italian journalists began yelling insults and were expelled before he could speak. In response, the [Romanian](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Romania "Kingdom of Romania") chairman, [Nicolae Titulescu](/wiki/Nicolae_Titulescu "Nicolae Titulescu"), jumped to his feet and shouted "Show the savages the door!" ("*À la porte les sauvages!*").[[141]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971133-143) Selassie denounced Italian aggression and criticised the world community for standing by. At the conclusion of his speech, which appeared on [newsreels](/wiki/Newsreel "Newsreel") throughout the world, he said "It is us today. It will be you tomorrow". France appeased Italy because it could not afford to risk an alliance between Italy and Germany; Britain decided that its military weakness meant that it had to follow France's lead.[[142]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTESalerno199766–104-144)[[143]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEHolt20111381–1401-145) Selassie's resolution to the League to deny recognition of the Italian conquest was defeated and he was denied a loan to finance a resistance movement.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971159-146) On 4 July 1936, the League voted to end the sanctions imposed against Italy in November 1935. By 15 July, the sanctions were at an end.[[145]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBaer1976299-147)[[c]](#cite_note-149)

On 18 November 1936, the [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire") was recognised by the [Empire of Japan](/wiki/Empire_of_Japan "Empire of Japan") and Italy recognised the Japanese occupation of [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria"), marking the end of the [Stresa Front](/wiki/Stresa_Front "Stresa Front").[[147]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELoweMarzari2010307-150)[[148]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcus199920-151) Hitler had supplied the Ethiopians with 16,000 rifles and 600 machine guns in the hope that Italy would be weakened when he moved against Austria.[[14]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeckie198764-15) By contrast, France and Britain recognised Italian control over Ethiopia in 1938. [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") was the only country to strongly condemn Italy's sovereignty over Ethiopia, respecting Ethiopian independence throughout. Including Mexico, only six nations in 1937 did not recognise the Italian occupation: [China](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%9349) "Republic of China (1912–49)"), New Zealand, the Soviet Union, [Republican Spain](/wiki/Second_Spanish_Republic "Second Spanish Republic") and the United States.[[149]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUSSD194328–32-152)[[150]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMarcus199922-153) Three years later, only the USSR officially recognised Selassie and the United States government considered recognising the Italian Empire with Ethiopia included.[[151]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELamb1999214-154) The invasion of Ethiopia and its general condemnation by Western democracies isolated Mussolini and Fascist Italy until 1938. From 1936 to 1939, Mussolini and Hitler joined forces to support the fascist camp during the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War"). In April 1939, Mussolini launched the [Italian invasion of Albania](/wiki/Italian_invasion_of_Albania "Italian invasion of Albania"). In May, Italy and Nazi Germany joined in the [Pact of Steel](/wiki/Pact_of_Steel "Pact of Steel"). In September 1940, both nations signed the [Tripartite Pact](/wiki/Tripartite_Pact "Tripartite Pact") along with the Empire of Japan.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The conflict has been described by historian Brenda Plummer as triggering the first "great manifestation" of [African American](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans") interest in foreign affairs, despite the financial limitations imposed by the [Great Depression](/wiki/Great_Depression "Great Depression") and the US government's interwar commitment to [neutrality](/wiki/Neutrality_Acts_of_the_1930s "Neutrality Acts of the 1930s").[[152]](#cite_note-155)[[153]](#cite_note-:2-156) Largescale organised responses to Italy's attack on Ethiopia included a peaceful demonstration in New York City on 18 August 1935 and Chicago on 1 September.[[153]](#cite_note-:2-156)[[154]](#cite_note-:3-157) Historian [Jacquelyn Dowd Hall](/wiki/Jacquelyn_Dowd_Hall "Jacquelyn Dowd Hall") situates these developments in her chronology of a "long civil rights movement" in which she traces the emergence of a black [popular front](/wiki/Popular_front "Popular front") in the 1930s.[[155]](#cite_note-158)

South Africa led by [J. B. M. Hertzog](/wiki/J._B._M._Hertzog "J. B. M. Hertzog") condemned the Italian invasion of Ethiopia and supported sanctions on Italy.[[156]](#cite_note-159)

## Aftermath

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Aftermath")]

### Italian occupation of Ethiopia, 1936–1940

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Italian occupation of Ethiopia, 1936–1940")]

Main articles: [Italian East Africa](/wiki/Italian_East_Africa "Italian East Africa") and [Italian Empire](/wiki/Italian_Empire "Italian Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Italian_East_Africa_%281938%E2%80%931941%29.svg/250px-Italian_East_Africa_%281938%E2%80%931941%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Italian_East_Africa_(1938%E2%80%931941).svg)

The six provinces of Italian East Africa

On 10 May 1936, Italian troops from the northern front and from the southern front met at Dire Dawa.[[106]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENicolle199712-108) The Italians found the recently released Ethiopian Ras, [Hailu Tekle Haymanot](/wiki/Hailu_Tekle_Haymanot "Hailu Tekle Haymanot"), who boarded a train back to Addis Ababa and approached the Italian invaders in submission.[[107]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971127-109) On 21 December 1937, Rome appointed [Amedeo, 3rd Duke of Aosta](/wiki/Amedeo,_3rd_Duke_of_Aosta "Amedeo, 3rd Duke of Aosta"), as the new Viceroy and Governor General of Italian East Africa with instructions to take a more conciliatory line.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/53/Guerillakrieg_in_Italienisch-Ostafrika_1937.png/250px-Guerillakrieg_in_Italienisch-Ostafrika_1937.png)](/wiki/File:Guerillakrieg_in_Italienisch-Ostafrika_1937.png)

Areas of [Arbegnoch](/wiki/Arbegnoch "Arbegnoch") resistance in 1937

Aosta instituted public works projects including 3,200 km (2,000 mi) of new paved roadways, 25 hospitals, 14 hotels, dozens of post offices, telephone exchanges, aqueducts, schools and shops. The Italians decreed [miscegenation](/wiki/Miscegenation "Miscegenation") to be illegal.[[157]](#cite_note-160) Racial separation, including residential segregation, was enforced as thoroughly as possible and the Italians showed favouritism to non-Christian groups.

To isolate the dominant [Amhara](/wiki/Amhara_people "Amhara people") rulers of Ethiopia, who supported Selassie, the Italians granted the [Oromos](/wiki/Oromos "Oromos"), the [Somalis](/wiki/Somali_people "Somali people") and other Muslims, many of whom had supported the invasion, autonomy and rights. The Italians also definitively abolished [slavery](/wiki/Abolition_of_slavery_timeline "Abolition of slavery timeline") and abrogated [feudal laws](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") that had been upheld by the Amharas. Early in 1938, a revolt broke out in Gojjam, led by the Committee of Unity and Collaboration, made up of some of the young, educated elite who had escaped reprisals after the assassination attempt on Graziani. The general oversaw another wave of reprisals and had all Ethiopians in administrative jobs murdered, some by being thrown from aircraft, after being taken on board under the pretext of visiting the King in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), leading to the saying "He went to Rome".[[158]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968281-161)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Amadeo_Aosta3rd_01.jpg/250px-Amadeo_Aosta3rd_01.jpg)](/wiki/File:Amadeo_Aosta3rd_01.jpg)

Duke of Aosta

The army of occupation had 150,000 men but was spread thinly; by 1941 the garrison had been increased to 250,000 soldiers, including 75,000 [Italian civilians](/wiki/Italians_of_Ethiopia "Italians of Ethiopia"). The former police chief of Addis Ababa, Abebe Aregai, was the most successful leader of the Ethiopian guerrilla movement after 1937, using units of fifty men. On 11 December, the [League of Nations](/wiki/League_of_Nations "League of Nations") voted to condemn Italy and Mussolini withdrew from the League.[[159]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968272-162) Along with world condemnation, the occupation was expensive, the budget for AOI from 1936 to 1937 required 19,136 billion [lire](/wiki/Lira "Lira") for infrastructure, when the annual revenue of Italy was only 18,581 billion lire.[[160]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECannistraro19825-163)

### East African campaign, 1940–1941

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: East African campaign, 1940–1941")]

Main article: [East African Campaign (World War II)](/wiki/East_African_Campaign_(World_War_II) "East African Campaign (World War II)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/The_West_African_Frontier_Force_in_East_Africa%2C_1941_E2003.jpg/250px-The_West_African_Frontier_Force_in_East_Africa%2C_1941_E2003.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_West_African_Frontier_Force_in_East_Africa,_1941_E2003.jpg)

Soldiers of the West African Frontier Force removing Italian frontier markers from the Kenya–Italian Somaliland border, 1941

While in exile in the United Kingdom, Haile Selassie had sought the support of the Western democracies for his cause but had little success until the Second World War began. On 10 June 1940, Mussolini declared war on France and Britain and attacked British and [Commonwealth](/wiki/Commonwealth_of_Nations "Commonwealth of Nations") forces in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt"), [Sudan](/wiki/Sudan "Sudan"), Kenya and [British Somaliland](/wiki/British_Somaliland "British Somaliland"). In August 1940, the [Italian conquest of British Somaliland](/wiki/Italian_conquest_of_British_Somaliland "Italian conquest of British Somaliland") was completed. The British and Selassie incited Ethiopian and other local forces to join a campaign to dislodge the Italians from Ethiopia. Selassie went to [Khartoum](/wiki/Khartoum "Khartoum") to establish closer liaison with the British and resistance forces within Ethiopia. On 18 January 1941, Selassie crossed the border into Ethiopia near the village of Um Iddla and two days later rendezvoused with [Gideon Force](/wiki/Gideon_Force "Gideon Force"). The Allied [East Africa Command](/wiki/East_Africa_Command "East Africa Command") and Ethiopian patriots had largely succeeded in their operations by 6 April 1941, when Addis Ababa was occupied by [Harry Wetherall](/wiki/Harry_Edward_de_Robillard_Wetherall "Harry Edward de Robillard Wetherall"), [Dan Pienaar](/wiki/Dan_Pienaar "Dan Pienaar") and [Charles Christopher Fowkes](/wiki/Charles_Christopher_Fowkes "Charles Christopher Fowkes"), who received the surrender of the city; on 5 May, exactly five years after the fall of the capital, Selassie made a formal entry in Addis Ababa.[[161]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1971156-164) After the Italian defeat, the [Italian guerrilla war in Ethiopia](/wiki/Italian_guerrilla_war_in_Ethiopia "Italian guerrilla war in Ethiopia") was carried out by remnants of Italian troops and their allies, which lasted until the [Armistice between Italy and Allied armed forces](/wiki/Armistice_between_Italy_and_Allied_armed_forces "Armistice between Italy and Allied armed forces") in September 1943.[[162]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTECernuschi199474-165)

### Peace treaty, 1947

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Peace treaty, 1947")]

Main article: [Treaty of Paris between Italy and the Allied Powers](/wiki/Treaty_of_Paris_between_Italy_and_the_Allied_Powers "Treaty of Paris between Italy and the Allied Powers")

The treaty signed in Paris by the [Italian Republic](/wiki/Italian_Republic "Italian Republic") (*Repubblica Italiana*) and the [victorious powers](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") of World War II on 10 February 1947, included formal Italian recognition of Ethiopian independence and an agreement to pay $25,000,000 (equivalent to $360,469,000 in 2025) in reparations. Since the League of Nations and most of its members had never officially recognized Italian sovereignty over Ethiopia, Haile Selassie had been recognized as the restored emperor of Ethiopia following his formal entry into Addis Ababa in May 1941. Ethiopia presented a bill to the Economic Commission for Italy of £184,746,023 for damages inflicted during the course of the Italian occupation. The list included the destruction of 2,000 churches, 535,000 houses, the slaughter or theft of 5,000,000 cattle, 7,000,000 sheep and goats, 1,000,000 horses and mules and 700,000 camels.[[18]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBarker1968292–293-19)

### Ethiopian War Crimes Commission, 1946

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Second_Italo-Ethiopian_War&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Ethiopian War Crimes Commission, 1946")]

On 20 May 1946, an Ethiopian War Crimes Commission was appointed to investigate and prosecute Italian war crimes committed during the Second Italo-Ethiopian War. The Ethiopian War Crimes Commission under [Ambaye Wolde Mariam](/wiki/Ambaye_Wolde_Mariam "Ambaye Wolde Mariam") began obtaining evidence to justify the trial of Marshal Badoglio and Marshal Graziani among other high ranking Italian officials.[[163]](#cite_note-:4-166)

Ethiopia’s efforts to submit cases to the [UN War Crimes Commission](/wiki/United_Nations_War_Crimes_Commission "United Nations War Crimes Commission") (UNWCC) raised a question of whether the Italo-Ethiopian conflict fell within its jurisdiction, as both Italian and British perspectives held that the war had effectively ended with Italy’s annexation by 1936—or at the latest before 1939—thereby framing any subsequent Italian violence as part of colonial administration rather than wartime conduct subject to UNWCC scrutiny.[[164]](#cite_note-167) Ethiopia’s Swedish legal adviser, Baron Erik Leijonhufvud, proposed a compromise in which an Ethiopian tribunal would include a majority of foreign judges and follow procedures and legal standards similar to those of the [Nuremberg International Military Tribunal](/wiki/Nuremberg_trials "Nuremberg trials").[[165]](#cite_note-168)[[166]](#cite_note-169)

Unlike in the Nuremberg Trials in post-Nazi Germany, few Italians were tried for war crimes after 1945. Critically, whatever the position taken by the UNWCC on individual cases, prosecution would require extradition of suspects, and Allied pressure on Italy to achieve this. Thus the Ethiopian War Crimes Commission limited itself to the prosecution of two persons, Badoglio and Graziani.[[163]](#cite_note-:4-166) However, Badoglio was never formally tried for war crimes, as the British government, in particular, saw Badoglio as a valuable asset for maintaining order in Italy.[[167]](#cite_note-170) Graziani was tried for Nazi collaborationism at the Supreme Court in 1948, but his colonial conduct was left unquestioned.[[168]](#cite_note-171)

Ethiopian veterans are seeking an apology and compensation from Italy for war crimes committed during the Second Italo-Ethiopian War, particularly the use of chemical weapons like mustard gas.[[169]](#cite_note-172) While there is no record of a formal, official apology, Italy did acknowledge the use of chemical weapons in a 1996 statement.[[170]](#cite_note-173)
